QF.id: SEBI Guidelines for AML Standards, CFT and PF with KYC Norms_4

question.text: What is Client Due Diligence (CDD) in the context of AML/KYC?

answer.text: Screening and verification carried out on an existing/prospective client using reliable and independent sources of identification
A one-time document collection process at the time of account opening
An annual audit conducted by SEBI on all intermediaries
A penalty imposed on clients who fail to provide documents

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** CDD is defined as "screening and verification carried out on an existing/prospective client using reliable and independent sources of identification." It is the act of performing background checks and other screening to ensure proper risk-assessment before onboarding. CDD is at the heart of AML and KYC initiatives.
**The Trap:** CDD is NOT a one-time process -- it is ongoing throughout the business relationship. It is NOT an audit by SEBI, and it is NOT a penalty. It is a continuous process of knowing and monitoring your client.
**Cross-Exam Context:** CDD is the most frequently tested concept across NISM exams. In III-A, V-A, and this exam, understanding CDD as an ongoing process (not a one-time check) is critical.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** CDD does not end after collecting documents. You must continuously monitor client transactions, update information periodically, and escalate any suspicious patterns. Think of CDD as a lifecycle, not an event.
